terminology_consistency.summary: Core terms (red-teaming, prompt injection, attacker, jailbreak, data leakage, context window, system prompt, user input, adversarial) are used identically across 5.5 and 5.6. 5.6 reuses 5.5's terms with explicit back-references ('In 5.5 you met the jailbreak'; 'You met data leakage in 5.5') and never redefines a clashing meaning.

prerequisites_respected.summary: Position order 5.5 (position_in_module 1) before 5.6 (position 2) is sane: test-your-own-system precedes a specific attack class. 5.5 defers prompt injection forward to 5.6; 5.6 lists 5.5 as prerequisite. Both correctly rely on earlier vocabulary (5.1 failures, 5.2 hallucination, 5.3 bias, 5.4 pillars, 3.2/3.4 LLM, 1.3 probabilistic). No forward references to later-position topics as prerequisites.

learning_arc.summary: The two topics form a coherent Safety Practices arc: 5.5 establishes proactive adversarial self-testing (the defensive practice); 5.6 drills into one concrete attack the testing hunts for, then closes the loop back to 5.5 ('that is why red teams keep testing for injection'). No gap or abrupt jump; module title (Safety Practices) and both topic titles are covered by the union of objectives.

no_duplicated_core_concepts.summary: Core Concepts sets are distinct: 5.5 covers red-team meaning/why-before-deploy/who/timeline/attack-categories/loop; 5.6 covers injection mechanism/why-it-works/direct-vs-indirect/impacts/vs-jailbreak. Jailbreak and data leakage are named in 5.5 and only re-referenced (not re-taught) in 5.6 via explicit back-pointers. Estimated noun-set Jaccard below the 0.6 flag threshold.

lab_activity_alignment.summary: Week-5 lab is a 500-word success+failure essay covering what went right/wrong, accountability, and what should change. Both topics support the 'what should change' / harm-prevention dimension (red-teaming as the proactive fix; injection defenses and human-in-the-loop). Each topic also carries a short hands-on chatbot exercise. Connection to the essay is thematic rather than a dedicated lab artifact, which the seed permits.

audience_level_consistency.summary: Both topics hold the absolute-beginner register and standard depth profile (per manifests). No later topic assumes a concept as known that no earlier topic introduced and that it fails to define.

findings.note: 'context window' is the load-bearing root-cause concept in 5.6 but appears nowhere earlier in this module (5.5 never uses it). 5.6 does define it inline on first use, so it passes; flagged only as the single arc concept lacking an earlier anchor.
'context window' introduced cold in 5.6 with no earlier-topic anchor; defined inline so non-blocking. Surfaces at HITL #4.

verdict: PASS — 5.5 and 5.6 form a coherent, non-duplicative safety-practices arc with consistent terminology, sane prerequisite ordering, and essay-lab support; one soft note (context window introduced cold in 5.6 but defined inline).
